title: Cost Of Distrust
summary: A pilgrim in Austin, Texas. Partly cloudy. Ninety-two degrees of dry heat. Three kilometers, and a check-in after a while away. They'd been heads-down building — deep in the Pilgrim app with Claude, turning a swarm of agents loose on the code until the tokens ran dry. About a model that launched and was pulled inside a week. About a tool closed to anyone who couldn't prove where they were born. About one country closing its source while another opens theirs, and who that leaves to set the direction. About Fight Club, a duvet, and the things we buy that we never really wanted. New projects, and the quiet cost of distrust.

title: The Inner Compass
summary: Two pilgrims in The Domain, Texas. Overcast and seventy-seven degrees. Three kilometers through a district of shops and open walkways. Two old friends, one who speaks in visions, in one long unbroken conversation. It opened with pilgrimage as the exit from life's normality. About sauntering instead of hiking, and the ground John Muir called holy. About wandering versus the destination, and how the detours hold the treasure. About GPS, and the part of the brain that forgets how to find its own way. About the inner compass — the inner CEO, the disruptive manager, the inner lobbyist. About mantras chanted a hundred thousand times, and guidance that arrives all at once. About small towns that turn the chains away, and a vision of AI as a bridge between worlds. Some walks are one long conversation about how to find the way.
